data.title: US Global Jets ETF: Why I Would Stay Away (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
This article is a follow-up analysis to the several airline reports that I have published in the past few days. Today, I will look at the U.S. Global Jets ETF (JETS), a fund that should reward (or punish) investors who choose to place a bet on the prospects of the U.S. airline business at large, without much concern for separating winners and losers within the peer group.

Below, I will explain my seemingly contradictory thesis. On the one hand, I believe that the US airline space is doing just fine as we cruise through the middle stages of the economic cycle, and I don't particularly see sizable risks on the horizon, other than the eventual short-term impact that events like the Middle East crisis could have on the industry. On the other hand, I don't necessarily believe that the tide will lift (or sink) all boats equally, which might make stock-picking in this sector more advisable.

About JETS

JETS is an ETF with AUM of about $700 million-plus that has been around since 2015. The fund trades an average volume of 4 million shares per day, according to Yahoo Finance, which amounts to roughly $100 million, making it look more like a trading vehicle rather than an investment vehicle. The management fee is a somewhat rich 60 bps per year.

As far as holdings are concerned, JETS distributed its assets across 55 airline-related holdings as of March 2026, although a mere ten of them received an allocation of just north of 60% of the total AUM. Maybe not a surprise, the Big 4 US airlines that include Southwest Airlines Co. (LUV), Delta Air Lines, Inc. (DAL), United Airlines Holdings, Inc. (UAL), and American Airlines Group Inc. (AAL) represent 42% of the fund (see pie chart below).

Finally, the largest non-US holding is Canadian airline Air Canada (ACDVF), at an allocation of 3.1%, and the largest non-North America holding is Spanish airport manager Aena S.M.E., S.A. (ANNSF), at an allocation of 1.1%.

Airline Space at Cruising Altitude

As I explained in my recent American Airlines article, airline revenues have been fairly well predicted on a 12-month lag, over the past three decades, by a few key leading economic indicators that speak to consumer behavior and the health of the so-called cyclical economy. The following indicators rank well on predictive power:

  • Initial unemployment claims.
  • Consumer expectations of future business conditions.
  • Building permits, to a lesser extent.

While the third one above has looked a bit soft since interest rates started to climb more decisively in early 2022, the broader picture is that the airline business seems to be in decent shape. To be clear, "decent" in this context refers more to a cruising-altitude state rather than the potential for substantially better capacity, occupancy, and per-unit prices ahead.

Above is a chart that plots projected US legacy airline revenue growth since the mid-1990s, based on the leading indicators above, against the historical reported figures (the chart "broke" a bit during the disruptive COVID-19 period). Notice how I currently expect the top line in this sector to grow by less than 5%, on average, over the next four quarters, suggesting business-as-usual conditions.

Aside from revenues, a dominating trend in the US airline sector since the pre-pandemic year of 2019 has been rising operating costs, excluding fuel, and compressing margins (see chart below). This has been due to skyrocketing personnel expenses during the high-inflation period of 2022-2025, aggravated by the need to ramp up airline capacity after the end of the COVID-19 lockdowns at virtually any cost, and a moderation in occupancy rates from the high levels of seven years ago.

Should the likes of Southwest Airlines, which is currently undergoing a business transformation process, manage to execute well on cost-saving initiatives as planned, some upside to earnings and to US airline stock prices could be captured without the need for materially higher passenger sales.

Picking Stocks Might Make More Sense

The above does not sound like a bearish or cautious view on the airline sector at all, which contradicts the title of this article. But I choose to set JETS aside and favor stock-picking in this case because, in my view and over a multi-year period, I believe that there will be relative winners and losers in the space.

The key criteria that I look at when choosing which companies to invest in (check out my recent coverage for a deeper dive) include the following:

  • Per-unit profitability: Here, I favor companies like DAL and UAL, as the former has consistently leveraged its strong hub position to pursue higher-margin domestic and international routes, and the latter has done a lot of heavy lifting in the past five years with a similar goal in mind.
  • Balance sheet robustness: I am much less comfortable investing in a heavily leveraged company within a space that is already sensitive to the economic cycles, which pushes me far away from the likes of AAL.
  • Proven business model: Airlines that have proven the efficacy of their business models over many years are more compelling to me than those that are still looking to reinvent themselves, which is the case with many low-cost carriers, including LUV.
  • Revenue diversification: Although a slightly less important item, I like to credit the likes of UAL and International Consolidated Airlines (ICAGY) for being well-positioned across geographically dispersed hubs that allow the companies to produce a more balanced mix of revenues from domestic vs. international destinations and economy vs. premium cabin passengers.

Therefore, I would be much more comfortable holding UAL, DAL, and ICAGY today than AAL, LUV, and even JETS itself. Therefore, I keep a "stay away" rating on the airline sector ETF, which is a downgrade from a few years ago, despite not necessarily being an outright bear on the space.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Iran War: One More Week Of Market Calm, Then Counting Down 100 Days To Tougher Times
data.description:
Investment thesis.

With the Iranian regime apparently having survived the initial shock of having a large swathe of its leadership now removed, it is now showing signs of resilience in fast decision-making and a willingness to turn this into a conflict of endurance. On one hand, it is completely outmatched militarily, and is under heavy attack, with little to show as an effective response in terms of inflicting military success. On the other hand, with its immediate and seemingly unwavering decision to close the Strait of Hormuz, it has effectively taken the global economy hostage.

As I shall explain, we probably have about a hundred days to find a way out of this before we risk a global economic implosion. At the same time, the market (SPY) will be largely aimless for another week or so, because if this war ends soon, it will have little impact from the market's perspective (not mentioning the human cost), therefore the market will still be pricing this possibility in. For now, I have the market as a hold, given my heavy allocation in oil and precious metals, which makes for a steady position going into this crisis. If this crisis lasts, I will switch to cautious, incremental bargain hunting.

Global Energy Markets are Facing Massive Disruption

It is day six of this conflict as I write this. With energy shipments through the Strait of Hormuz disrupted, the world probably lost about 100 million barrels of oil in supplies so far. It should be noted, however, that much of this oil will likely be found in regional storage facilities, and they will be tapped after the war. If it remains completely shut for the next 100 days, we could be looking at a loss of roughly 1 to 2 billion barrels in supplies. The world is also set to lose about 60 Bcm of LNG for the period. The latter should not produce widespread global effects, but rather some degree of shortages for some nations. India, for instance, has already taken measures to ration natural gas supplies headed for its industry.

I should clarify that my 100-day assessment is not for when we start experiencing economic turbulence. That is the approximate threshold where I see high chances of systemic risks, resulting from economic disruptions starting to create ripple effects, where even if we see an end to this war soon after this threshold is reached, it will be too late to prevent severe consequences from occurring, with wide-reaching effects potentially lasting for years.

Few Good Options to Stop this War

Just over two decades ago, the US confidently engaged in the Iraq regime change operation, however afterwards, the Sunni and Shia began to also attack the US presence. Iraq's neighbors ended up adding fuel the war by backing their own interests through various militias.

The current Iran regime change bears some similar hallmarks to what took place in Iraq. Assumptions that regime change would happen immediately after the removal of its leadership were clearly wrong. The Iranian regime is cornered, but not gone. It has no choice but to do what has always been feared would happen in the event of such a conflict. It decided to take the global economy hostage through the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, thus making the passage of time its best and arguably only true ally.

A land incursion to secure perhaps a few thousand square kilometers around the Strait of Hormuz might help to diminish the threat to shipping in the region, but it might not eliminate Iran's ability to target ships. There is no telling what unforeseen consequences such an operation might have in terms of losses; it will not be an easy decision to make.

There is also the option to widen the conflict by bringing in Iran's neighbors, such as Azerbaijan (via the arming of ethnic Azeris in Iran), or the UAE, which already stated a desire to retaliate against Iran's targeting of its territory due to US bases there. The problem with that is that Iran may likely respond by destroying the oil infrastructure of the UAE, as well as Azeri oil and gas infrastructure, if it gets involved, which then leaves us with oil supply disruptions that will last far beyond this war. Widening the conflict also has the potential for it to get out of control, and it could potentially last for years, much like the 2003 Iraq war did.

There is always the option of ending this war by simply calling it a mission accomplished, citing the destruction of missile stockpiles and production facilities as objectives achieved. If this is the path already chosen, we will likely see it happen in the next two weeks or so. The MSM will probably give the Trump administration a pass, in the name of the greater good, by not dwelling too much on the failure to achieve the original stated goal of regime change. The risk may be that Iran may unexpectedly not back down from its blockade of Strait of Hormuz, and set some demands, such as sanctions relief, before it does so.

Investment Implications

As the first week of war comes to an end, I executed trades amounting to about 2% of my portfolio, mostly as a cash-generating exercise, in expectation of bargain-hunting opportunities down the line. About a fifth of my portfolio is in cash. About a quarter of my stock portfolio is in oil & gas-related stocks, and those stocks are mostly up this week, but I do not deem the gains we have seen so far significant enough to warrant taking profits. The rest of the market has mostly responded aimlessly so far this week. No buying opportunity has emerged from my perspective thus far that makes it enticing enough within the context of where this conflict could be headed, with related risks and opportunities.

I expect more of the same from the markets going into next week. For this market to gain direction, we need physical proof of the consequences. That proof will arrive in about two weeks in the form of those oil and LNG tankers that were about to go through the Strait of Hormuz for pickup in the past few days, not delivering the volumes of oil and gas to the expected destinations. In the meantime, there ares still many vessels out there lining up for delivery.

Europeans, for instance, will likely come to the realization that as long as this conflict continues, they will have a hard time replenishing their depleted natural gas inventories for next winter. Their inventories are currently at the second-lowest level this decade.

With far fewer seaborne oil deliveries arriving to destined markets starting in a few weeks, the physical reality of inventory drawdowns will start to hit home. Petrochemical products such as fertilizers, liquid fuels, and chemical precursors to plastics will not make it to their destination either. This is where ripple effect calculations will enter the equation.

If farmers around the world do not have enough chemical fertilizer inputs, we can start thinking of food price inflation going into next year. Qatar, for instance, exports about one tenth of the world's urea. Inflation of energy prices, such as gasoline, will be felt in the economy immediately at the pump. Inflation in industrial and agricultural inputs will be felt down the line, setting us up for inflationary pressures for months or even years to come.

Roughly 100 days into this war, we are likely to see severe, but localized physical shortages throughout the global economy becoming widespread. Based on EIA data, countries like Italy, France, and Germany only have about 75-100 days' worth of oil & products stockpiled. Japan, which is heavily reliant on Middle Eastern oil imports, as well as LNG, will probably have to start aggressively rationing both oil and gas within about two months. It has an oil stockpile of 500 million barrels as of November of last year.

Even the US, although it is a net exporter of natural gas and only imports about 15% of its total oil and products needs, can suffer from a severe energy price shock, especially if measures are not taken to restrict exports. The cost of derived products such as fertilizers, plastics, and so on can rise, regardless of whether energy prices rise together with that of the global markets or not. The US Federal Reserve will probably feel compelled to act on rising inflation risks, in tandem with the world's other major central banks.

With these factors in mind, there are some broader considerations, as well as industry &and stock specific considerations that need to be weighed. It will take until next week at least for us to get a better sense of whether this conflict lasts or not. If a week from now, we can reasonably assume that both sides are entrenched for a long fight, then we need to consider the worst-case scenario, namely a conflict with no end in sight. This means that an oil supply shortfall of about 10-20 mb/d will have to be resolved through a global demand destruction event.

At around 100 days into this war, I expect we will see the markets starting to price in such an economic calamity, since actual physical shortages ranging from gasoline to chemicals will emerge all over the world. Oil producer stocks will probably ride this demand destruction event higher in its initial oil price spike phase. When we start to see a rebalancing of the supply/demand equation, they will probably sell off. The rebalancing could potentially lead to supply destruction overshoot, at which point oil prices could plunge dramatically, even as supplies continue to be shut in.

Most other market segments, except perhaps for gold and silver, are likely to see very steep selloffs as oil prices start to rise into demand destruction territory. With this in mind, I intend to be careful in pursuing bargains. True bargains may only arise when oil prices reach new all-time highs, beating the old record of 2008.

Some of the specific stocks I am looking at all have particular profiles that have the potential to make them relative winners within this context.

Equinor ASA(EQNR) is well-positioned to take advantage of oil and natural gas prices potentially rising at a faster pace in Europe than elsewhere, such as in the US and China. It is Europe's largest natural gas provider, and it also produces most of its oil in Norway, which is mostly sold on the European market.

Dow Inc. (DOW) has the potential to benefit from being a mostly US-based chemicals company. Natural gas prices are likely to diverge even more than they already are between the US market and more expensive markets such as Europe. Dow Inc. could potentially become a major chemical products exporter to markets that are experiencing severe natural gas supply disruptions as a result of Middle Eastern LNG exports being shut in. Those market share gains could become permanent if the war is prolonged.

I am somewhat positive on traditional defensive stocks such as Kimberly-Clark Corporation (KMB) or The Kraft-Heinz Corporation (KHC). Their input costs should increase significantly, but at the same time, I believe that they have enough pricing power to pass on at least some of the cost increases to the consumer. These are stocks I am looking to hold through this crisis.

Health stocks such as Abbott Laboratories (ABT) may fare alright, as long as this conflict ends at some point within the next few months. Beyond that, we could potentially reach a point where enough people would be left to make increasingly tough choices on whether they buy food or spend on health care needs, to the point where hospitals, labs, and other facilities may stop buying supplies.

I continue to see gold and silver as important safe-haven assets, despite what the first trading days since the start of this war suggest.

As I already stated, I would not put a lot of stock into making assumptions on market direction based on the first few days of trading since the war started. I am looking to buy gold and silver stocks on a sizable dip. I already have a very robust precious metals position in my portfolio, which is why I am not rushing to buy as this war unfolds.

Risks to my Thesis

My main strategy of mostly staying put for the first two weeks of this war might lead to many opportunities missed, as well as a chance to unload stocks that could face dramatic selloffs in the coming weeks. If the war ends very soon, I may miss out on taking profits on my sizable oil and gas positions. There is also a high probability that we will start seeing the broader stock market sell off dramatically starting next week, so it may make some sense to reduce one's positions in certain stocks now, rather than later, if one is looking to reposition in expectation of a broad market selloff.

Conclusions

The latest news is that Iran switched from a blanket closure of the Strait to a selective access system, where ships signaling that they operate for China, for instance, are given a pass. If this is the case, and perhaps only ships intended for supplying US-friendly states like Japan or most of Europe are being stopped, the net effect on the global economy might be a significant delay of my 100-day timeline to absolute disaster.

At the same time, it signals that Iran has more than just a sporadic ability to threaten anything that moves through the Strait. If this is the case, we could be in for a very long standoff, and whether we reach the severe global economic crisis point in 100 days or 200 days, we are headed there, and as investors, we need to prepare accordingly. Until we see a bit more clarity on whether we are in for a long war or not, there is a case to be made for not rushing to trade just yet, even though it does come with some risk. We should get more clarity next week.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SL Green Realty: Pending Dividend Cut As Occupancy Ramps Higher
data.description:
SL Green Realty Corp. (SLG), which has dipped by 33.4% over the last year, is set to change the frequency of its previously monthly dividends to every quarter and is likely set to bring in a dividend cut. The Manhattan office-focused REIT has seen its occupancy rate recover on the back of strong demand for Class A office space in the Borough, with its fiscal 2025 fourth-quarter same-store occupancy rising by 60 basis points to 93.0% from 92.4% in the third quarter. This is inclusive of leases signed but not yet commenced, and helped the REIT generate fourth-quarter revenue of $276.47 million. This was up 12.4% over its year-ago comp, with the REIT generating funds from operations ("FFO") of $1.13 per share. Funds from operations, or FFO, on a nominal basis came in at $86.2 million, down from $131.9 million in the year-ago period, with the per-share figure also dipping from $1.81 per share.

Critically, while the year-ago comp benefited from around $0.36 per share in gains on discounted debt extinguishments, gains in occupancy have not been translated to FFO strength. This forms the core of the bearish case against SLG. FFO weakness during the fourth quarter was catalyzed by a net loss of $104.6 million, around $1.49 per share. This was a significant deterioration from a net income of $9.4 million, or $0.13 per share, in the year-ago quarter. The drivers of the net income dip are important, as the bulk of these items were non-cash. The REIT did see quarterly interest expenses jump by around $11 million to $49.4 million. Total operating expenses only went up by $25.35 million, with the divergence seen in the large net loss figure mainly coming from unfavorable comps from equity gains and depreciable real estate reserves.

Prior to the aforementioned dividend frequency change, SLG last declared a monthly cash dividend of $0.2575 per share. This was kept unchanged from the prior payment, and $3.09 per share was annualized for a 7.5% dividend yield. The REIT is yet to communicate what the new quarterly dividend will be, but I'd expect a cut from its current level, even with coverage at 146%.

Balance Sheet and the Preferreds

SLG was most recently rated "BB+" this month by Fitch Ratings, just below the benchmark for investment grade, with this rating in place since the summer of 2023, when it was downgraded. The REIT faces around $1.55 billion in total maturing debt this year. The joint venture portion of this total, around $895 million, is held at an average interest rate of 4.33%. This is currently just 21 basis points over the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y), which is low and implies that they will have to refinance this debt at a higher rate. There was a total cash and cash equivalent of $155.7 million as of the end of the fourth quarter, which means they will either have to push through property sales or tap new debt refinancing to meet the debt maturities.

The REIT closed on 56 office leases during the fourth quarter, with this spread across 766,783 square feet and at a mark-to-market that was 6.4% higher than prior fully escalated rents on the same spaces.

The 6.50% Series I Cumulative Preferreds (SLG.PR.I) have seen weakness since the start of the year, dropping by around 4% as they're currently trading for $21.89 per share. These pay out a $1.63 per share annual coupon for what currently works out to be 6 basis points of income below the common share dividend against a yield on cost of 7.44%. However, it's highly likely there will be another dividend cut once SLG announces its new quarterly dividend. This sets up the preferreds as a safer choice for consistent income for more risk-averse investors. These provide a healthy spread over long-term Treasuries and are also trading at a 12.44% discount to their $25 per share redemption value.

Conclusion

While there has been some uncertainty around continued corporate demand for office space in New York following Mayoral changes and the floated tax increase, these fears have largely proved to be wrong, with Manhattan now having the lowest vacancy rate in the U.S. at 13.1% as of the end of January. Demand for Class office space continues to heat up, with office-to-residential conversions working hand in hand with this to reduce the supply of office space and boost the spread they earn on new leases. SLG's sentiment is close to lows, with 14.60% of its shares outstanding currently being sold short. I am rating SLG as a Hold until broader geopolitical headwinds abate.

data.title: Blue Owl: Private Credit Concerns Make Me Wary
data.description:
Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL) has had very weak share price performance over the last year while its underlying business performance has remained good, but this doesn’t seem to be an opportunity due to the rising risk of a credit crisis ahead. This makes its investment case quite binary and risky, making me cautious as a long-term investor that makes capital preservation a long-term goal for my portfolio.

Business Overview

Blue Owl Capital is an alternative asset manager with some $307 billion of assets under management (AuM) at the end of 2025. Its business is mainly distributed across three areas, namely Credit, Real Assets, and General Partners (GP) Strategic Capital. About 51% of its AuMs are in private credit, 26% in Real Assets, and the rest is in strategic capital.

The company is a leading asset manager in the private credit sector, making Blue Owl one of the main partners for private companies across direct lending and alternative credit markets, plus it also manages several Business Development Companies (BDC). Due to its relatively large size and business profile, its main competitors include other alternative asset managers, such as Blackstone (BX) or Apollo Global Management (APO), and other BDCs, like Ares Capital (ARCC) or Main Street Capital (MAIN).

Its customer base is diversified across private clients, insurance companies, pension funds, sovereign wealth funds, and others. At the end of last year, about 60% of AuMs were from institutional investors, while 40% were from private customers, with the vast majority (around 75%) being geographically based in North America.

Its AUM has increased at very fast growth rates in recent years, from about $62 billion when it was listed to more than $300 billion nowadays, both through organic initiatives and acquisitions. Moreover, the company has also diversified the business across private credit, real estate, data center financing, and others, which has also been an important factor for higher AuMs in recent years.

This rapid growth has been supported by secular demand for alternative asset classes, as investors are increasingly allocating more capital into higher-yielding investment solutions, and tighter banking regulation following the global financial crisis of 2008-09 has forced banks to step aside from more risky credit loans. This is a trend that is not expected to slow down over the coming years, both from the customer demand side (especially regarding institutional investors) and from banking regulation, boding well for Blue Owl’s growth prospects over the medium-to-long term.

As the company’s business model is to collect recurring fees mainly based on AuMs, the rapid increase in assets under management has been a key factor for higher revenues and earnings in recent years. As shown in the next graph, Blue Owl has a very good growth history over the past few years, with top-line increasing at around 30% annually over the past five years.

In 2025, about 85% of its management fees were driven by permanent capital investment vehicles with long-term investment horizons, which means these are revenues that are largely predictable and should be relatively stable over the long term.

Investors should note that not all AuMs are fee-generating. Thus, the most important metric for its revenue base is FPAUM (fee-paying assets under management), which amounted to around $187 billion at the end of 2025, representing about 61% of total AuMs. These FPAUM are the assets under management that generate fee-related revenues (FRE) and earnings, which are more recurring and provide Blue Owl with a somewhat predictable revenue and earnings base over the long term.

As the company continues to expand its product range and achieve net inflows from investors, Blue Owl aims to more than double its AuMs, fees, and earnings over the next few years. This shows that management is clearly confident about the company’s growth prospects over the next few years, expecting to have more than $500 AuMs by 2029.

Despite strong business growth in recent years, Blue Owl’s credit quality has remained strong, as the company’s approach to credit underwriting can be considered conservative, both related to direct lending and real assets financing. Its annualized loss ratios across both private credit strategies and real estate credit have been quite low in recent years, being a strong signal that lending criteria are sound and Blue Owl is not chasing riskier borrowers to grow its business.

Having said that, there have been increasing market concerns about private credit exposures recently, and Blue Owl is obviously not immune to those fears. Assuming the company has not changed its underwriting criteria much in recent years, its credit losses should remain at manageable levels ahead, but there have been some cracks in the private credit segment in recent months that have raised some questions about ‘true’ credit quality in this space.

Credit Concerns

While Blue Owl’s track record and growth trajectory have been quite solid in the recent past, this has not been reflected in its share price and valuation over the past few months. As shown in the next graph, Blue Owl’s shares have declined by close to half over the last twelve months, showing a great disconnect between its underlying fundamentals and share price.

This means Blue Owl may be a great opportunity for investors right now, or, on the other hand, there are some risks that the market is already pricing in that aren’t yet reflected in its underlying financial performance.

Indeed, looking at historical financial figures, Blue Owl has reported a positive operating performance, with fees and distributable earnings being up by double digits during 2025. This was supported by positive investment performance across its investment classes, acquisitions, and positive equity fundraising.

Moreover, at the end of last year, Blue Owl had more than $28 billion in AuMs not yet generating fees, which have the potential to generate annual fees of about $326 million when deployed, being a potential boost for its management fees by double digits over the coming quarters.

Despite this overall positive background, the market has become increasingly concerned about private credit exposures following some recent credit events that have negatively impacted mainly the banking sector, as I’ve recently covered on Wells Fargo (WFC).

The defaults of Tricolor Holdings and First Brands some months ago raised concerns about potential issues in the private credit segment, while more recently the insolvency of Market Financial Solutions in the U.K. was another credit event raising fears of a potential credit downcycle ahead. While these isolated events seem to be manageable and have impacted mainly the banking sector, they have increased investors’ concerns about private credit due to its rapid growth in recent years and lack of regulation.

Furthermore, these credit events were not worrying due to similar business models or concerns about a specific industry; rather, they had similarities about some management practices, including opaque structures, aggressive leverage, fraud, and misrepresentation by management.

As the private credit segment is not very transparent to outside investors, this has raised concerns about weak lending underwriting criteria and issues related to governance. Plus, the rapid growth of private credit over the past few years has likely resulted in weaker due diligence from lenders in recent years, potentially not boding well for credit quality ahead.

The Tricolor default was also especially worrying because it was related to asset-backed financing, which usually is considered safer than other types of financing. Tricolor performed fraud by double-pledging collateral and manipulating data, being quite concerned about the due diligence performed by lenders, raising doubts about general credit quality across private credit.

Given these events and signs of bad practices across private credit, which usually is less transparent and lacks public prices and information, investors have re-priced risk for companies exposed to these types of borrowers. Not surprisingly, one segment that was particularly hit was BDCs, of which Blue Owl was no exception, and this explains to some extent its weak share price performance in recent months.

On top of that, in recent months the market has also become more worried about the private credit sector due to its exposure to software companies and the AI build-up cycle.

Companies are using higher levels of leverage to finance capital expenditures related to AI investments, namely data centers and chip purchases, with large hyperscalers like Amazon.com (AMZN) or Microsoft (MSFT) having strong balance sheets to support this, but other smaller companies like CoreWeave (CRWV) are using high levels of leverage to finance capex, which may be troubling ahead.

While this doesn’t seem to be an immediate issue, it’s raising questions about credit quality in the future, being negative for investor sentiment toward the private credit sector.

More worrying seems to be the potential impact on software companies of agentic AI, which has evolved rapidly in recent months and seems to be a threat to the business model of software companies, both small and large.

Given that the private credit sector has been quite active financing leveraged buyouts (LBOs) in recent years and is more exposed to smaller companies in the software market that may be more at risk from disruption from agentic AI, investors have been increasingly worried about potential credit losses in the near future.

While default rates have supposedly remained at low levels in the recent past, the default rate based on the Proskauer Private Credit Default Index increased to 2.46% in Q4 2025, up from about 1.76% six months ago, according to Morningstar. This increase seems manageable, but this does not take into account the ‘extend and pretend’ practices in the sector, including restructurings, distressed exchanges, and liability management exercises, that push the ‘true’ default rate closer to a level of 5%.

This means loss rates are likely to maintain an upward trend ahead, especially if software companies start to show weakness over the coming quarters, with a potential credit crisis in a worst-case scenario a possibility over the next two to three years.

Given Blue Owl’s business profile and high exposure to private credit, the company is naturally impacted by these concerns, both indirectly through negative investor sentiment and also due to its direct exposure to software companies.

Reportedly, Blue Owl’s software exposure represents between 8% and 11% of its portfolio. While credit quality has remained good so far and the loan-to-value ratios are about 30% on average for technology exposures, which is a conservative level, the risk is that AI will disrupt the business model of several software-as-a-service (SaaS) companies and lead to higher default rates ahead. Some analysts are talking about private credit default rates hitting levels like 15% in case of severe AI disruption, which is obviously negative for a company like Blue Owl.

This background has not been good for companies and funds highly exposed to private credit, leading to redemptions from investors and some liquidity issues recently. Blue Owl had to halt redemptions from some of its funds targeted for retail investors, which is not a good sign for investors and makes future fundraising more difficult.

This does not bode well for its growth prospects ahead, potentially putting at risk its business goals for the next few years, even though so far Blue Owl continues to be able to report net inflows. It seems early to comprehend the impact of recent events on the company’s business prospects and loss rates ahead, but in my opinion the background is not positive, and it will be just a matter of time to be negatively reflected in Blue Owl’s operating performance.

This explains, in my opinion, why Blue Owl’s valuation has de-rated considerably in recent months, trading right now at only about 11x earnings, which seems to be relatively low compared to its historical levels. Moreover, the company is also offering a high-dividend yield around 8.7%, but this seems to be more a trap than an opportunity for investors in my opinion.

Conclusion

Blue Owl Capital has a good track record and growth history in recent years, but cracks in the private credit sector are a warning sign that problems may lie ahead. As transparency and credit disclosures are rather weak across the sector for outside investors, investor sentiment has naturally turned negative in recent months as a potential credit crisis may be brewing in the private credit sector. This means Blue Owl’s investment case seems to be quite binary right now, having significant upside if credit conditions don’t deteriorate much over the next few years or potentially having a lot more pain ahead if a credit crisis happens in the near future. Given this background, personally I prefer to stay away from its stock for the time being, as Blue Owl seems to be a high-risk play right now in the financial sector.

data.title: International Airlines Group: Downgraded To Buy On Fuel Risk
data.description:
International Consolidated Airlines Group (ICAGY) (BABWF) stock price has increased nearly 60% since my last report, which is extremely close to my price target set for 2025.

In my prior report, I already noted the geopolitical risk, and we now see that playing out:

While IAG has been able to efficiently extract value from the market, we do note that geopolitical tension such as war and trade may somewhat pressure the strong ability to extract value from the market efficiently.

Given that there is some uncertainty in the markets due to the situation in Iran, which does affect oil prices, which is indirectly one of the major input costs for airlines, I believe it is a good moment to revisit investment prospects of IAG, as the stock has now risen 265% since I initiated coverage with a buy rating in July 2022 and uncertainty is higher than in the years prior.

Jet Fuel Prices Head Higher

The main risk for IAG is the same as for other airlines, as I outlined in a separate report. Higher oil prices are a near-direct pressure on airline margins, while any softening in demand, either through macro conditions shifting or fear of flying, could also soften unit revenues. Bloomberg has reported that jet fuel prices surged nearly 50% in Europe in just two days. It quite well shows the risk that airlines may face on their financials.

While US airlines have largely stepped away from jet fuel hedging. IAG is still doing it as discussed in their earnings release:

The Group seeks to reduce the impact of volatile commodity prices by hedging prices in advance. The Group’s fuel hedging policy is designed to provide flexibility to respond to both significant unexpected reductions in travel demand or capacity and/or material or sudden changes in jet fuel prices. The policy allows for differentiation within the Group, to match the nature of each operating company, and the use of call options for a proportion of the hedging undertaken. The policy operates on a three-year rolling basis, with hedging of up to 75% of anticipated requirements in near-term, reducing steadily over the first two years down to 20% for the final year. The policy also provides flexibility for low-cost airlines within the Group to adopt hedging of up to 80% in the near-term.

The hedging policy is to typically be up to 75% hedged for the first year and even 80% for low-cost airlines, and as we see with basically all hedging policies, outyears fuel hedging as a percentage of expected usage are lower, towards 20% in year three. So, there is an impact, but only on around 25% of the fuel costs. Effectively that would mean that fuel costs currently are around 11.7% higher against the undisturbed jet fuel price. However, the company had 62% hedged, meaning the impact is almost 18%.

Lower Fuel Costs Drove IAG Earnings In 2025

In 2025, IAG’s revenues grew 3.5%, including 2.5% growth in passenger revenues and 16% growth in other revenues. We note that passenger revenues increased in line with the 2.4% capacity expansion, showing that unit revenues were more or less stable. Passenger revenues were still the main growth driver of revenues, but it is evident that there hasn’t been any upside on unit revenues. Other revenues drove nearly 40% of the growth as loyalty program, maintenance, and ground handling revenues increased.

Operating profit increased 13.1%, or €581 million, driven by a €525 million decrease in fuel costs. Putting it differently, 90% of the profit increase was driven by lower fuel costs, and that does show the pressure for airlines even with fuel hedges in place.

Currently, it is extremely difficult to assess what the impact of higher prices will be, as we need to see for how long prices will remain elevated. However, if we were to apply an 18% increase in blended fuel cost for 2025, we would see that operating profit would be €3.75 billion, driving a 380-basis point margin pressure. So this quite well shows the sensitivity of margins to jet fuel prices.

IAG Downgraded To Buy, But There May Be Upside

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

The stock price target is based on the listing of IAG at the London Stock Exchange converted to USD, as the home listing provides better volumes, allowing for better price making and buying and selling at desired prices and quantities. Other ways to invest in International Airlines Group are through the OTC tickers ICAGY (representing two ordinary shares) and BABWF (representing one ordinary share).

While I would like to be able to say that we can model the impact of higher fuel prices. The reality is that we cannot. There is simply too much unknown to be able to make a fully informed estimate on it. However, we can model a 3-point drag to the margins and see what that does for the valuation case of IAG. We note that the assumption is the roughly four-point impact we noted against 2025 earnings minus one point of recovery that is typically possible.

This has also been cross-verified through calculations with IAG’s guidance already assuming a €400 million impact solely from the curve shifting between the end of 2025 and the reporting date. The average hedged fuel price was $751 per metric ton. The new fuel price is roughly $449 per metric ton higher. This brings the blended price to $922 per metric ton. Converting to euros and multiplying by the fuel volume that would drive a €1.7 billion impact with a net of a 40% recovery through pass-through would be €1 billion and around €0.9 billion, as roughly two months have already been executed in 2026. That would bring a roughly 280 basis points headwind, which I believe can be rounded to 3 points. Due to lower hedging for 2027, the impact for 2027 would be more than twice as large.

If we implement that into the price targets, we note that there is 9% downside from current prices and 29% downside for the stock price target for 2027. However, I believe that the surge to $1,200 may not stick. A more realistic scenario in my view may be that the price goes to $1,000 or even $900.

If fuel prices go to $1,000, we see that upside will be around 7%, with 2027 offering little to no upside against the projected target for 2026.

If we set the prices at $900, there remains around 12% upside for 2026 and 21% upside for 2027. I believe this does provide the more balanced scenario, and I set a price target of $5.59 or $11.18 per ICAGY share (presenting two ordinary shares), providing 12% upside and $6.01 or $12 with forward pricing.

The risk is obviously that fuel prices remain elevated. However, I do believe that the scenario of a $900 target in the near term is more reflective of reality. While my monitor does show a strong buy rating at $900 per metric ton. I do feel the uncertainty warrants a downgrade from strong buy to buy.

Conclusion: Fuel Price Is Definitely A Risk But I Do Not Expect Current High Prices To Stick

Fuel prices can definitely erode margins, and more often than not, that is why airline stocks are volatile. High fuel prices erode margins more than they create revenue opportunities, while very low fuel prices may be signs of economic distress that can affect the top line. Airlines benefit from a situation somewhere in between where oil prices are low but not low due to a fundamentally challenging macro picture that may affect travel demand. For IAG, I do believe that there is indeed a risk, but I also do not believe that the current fuel price situation will stick. Therefore, I do downgrade the stock to buy, but I currently do not see a strong reason to downgrade to hold or even sell.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 2 Retirement Yield Picks That Give You The Right To Win
data.description:
In management consulting, which is my background, there is a common question that consultants and corporate leaders try to address together before embarking on new capital allocation:

Does your strategy give you the right to win?

It is a holistic question, which forces one to take both a helicopter view (geography, sector, market share) and deep-dives (various capabilities, balance sheet, product) to understand whether the business stands a good chance to deliver superior long-term returns by doing what it has been doing so far or by venturing into new areas.

The idea is that businesses have to earn their right to win.

It can be earned by making choices that are aligned with capabilities/strengths - i.e., "playing on your strengths".

Now, how does this fit together with durable and/or retirement income investing? Here are my thoughts.

In durable income investing the concept of winning is a combination of

a) stable current income streams,

b) tangible yields,

c) compounding at a meaningful tempo.

When thinking about whether the investment strategy gives us the right to win, we have to earn it by aligning our choices (picks) with the businesses that have durable cash flows, predictability and smooth compounding (growth) as their strengths.

Plus, we have to make sure that these items are really their strengths and not temporary features due to, say, favorable market conditions or the right cycle.

The recent equity CLO (OXLC), weaker private credit (FSCO) and aggressive yield covered call ETF product (ULTY) NAV (and yield) declines serve as good proof. And this can also happen in optically de-risked segments of the market like investment grade telcos (T) and equity REITs (ARE).

Let me now present two investment picks, which, in my view, embody all of the critical elements that should give you, as a durable income investor, the right to win (i.e., compounding cash flows in a smooth and stress-free fashion).

Two 5%+ yield picks that give you the right to win in your retirement portfolio

Effectively, the notion of winning is about maximizing yield and its growth without experiencing value/income-destructive bumps in the process.

Enbridge Inc. (ENB) - midstream infrastructure - and Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN) - a business development company/private credit vehicle - have what it takes.

So, let's start with the first and the easiest aspect, which is the yield.

ENB and MAIN provide a dividend yield of 5.2% and 7.3%, respectively. These are not sky-high levels, but definitely something that does not introduce a drag on income compounding.

Now, and this is where the substance lies, the underlying fundamentals are so robust that the yield per unit of risk is truly attractive.

Both players have managed to distribute consistent dividend for decades and across different market regimes, which I think could be treated as a positive sign:

Neither ENB nor MAIN have cut their dividends since their IPO dates, which has happened before the GFC. Instead, both businesses have gradually expanded the distributions, while delivering juicy price appreciation.

In the chart, looking at MAIN, we will notice some periodic spikes (both to the upside and downside). These are special distributions that go over base dividends. Usually, when the market conditions go south, MAIN tends to trim down special dividends in order to safeguard its balance sheet. Conversely, when it manages to monetize some of its equity investment portfolio, it rewards its income investors, accordingly.

Here we can see how huge role the special dividends have played for MAIN (just look at MAIN's total return vs. its price return):

Having said that, ENB and MAIN offer something much more important and valuable in the context of durable dividend generation than just almost perfect history.

For ENB the case is very straightforward. It runs a utility-like business, where the cash flows are underpinned by mostly long-term fee-based and inflation-linked contracts, which are stipulated by IG grade offtakers.

And the infrastructure we are talking about here is gas transmission, gas storage, gas distribution, liquid pipes and renewable power plants, which almost all are backed by long-term power purchase agreements. Positioning-wise ENB's portfolio is constructed almost perfectly, allowing the shareholders to participate in the AI expansion through the reliable baseload power angle.

The fact that ENB pays out only 60% to 70% of distributable cash flows and have the organic growth supported by the fee-based contracts, which provide close to 2% annual income growth create a favorable ground for organic long-term compounding.

For MAIN, the business model does not look as great as for ENB, at least not on the surface. It is a private credit machine, which already is enough to scare away a large chunk of investors.

However, the devil is in the details. And right because of these details, MAIN has been able to deliver consistent dividends without NAV erosion for about 2 decades in a row.

And here I would like to underscore 5 points, which are vital to neutralize some unjustified biases around MAIN:

First, MAIN has a diversified portfolio with no notable exposure to SaaS, which has been the key driver for the recent BDC (BIZD) selloff:

Second, MAIN invests in lower middle market companies, where there is weaker competition from the private credit giants such as KKR (KKR) and Blue Owl (OWL) simply because of the scale issue. As a result of this, MAIN can access greater demand from which to cherry pick the right borrowers, while being strict on the covenant side.

Third, MAIN's portfolio companies carry conservative capital structures and are far away from being speculative. For example, on average, the senior leverage of its portfolio company stands only at 2.4x. The EBITDA to senior interest coverage is at 3.0x. These are one of the strongest metrics in the BDC arena.

Fourth, MAIN injects equity capital along with its private credit finance in order to secure some equity-like upside potential. Once these stakes get monetized, MAIN can usually capture sizeable returns, which can both offset credit losses and accommodate juicy special distributions (as I indicated above). For instance, as of Q4, 2025, MAIN had ~$7.10 per share in cumulative, pre-tax net unrealized appreciation, which corresponds to ~12% of its market cap.

Fifth, MAIN has the third least leveraged balance sheet in the BDC world. The debt to equity is 0.73x, while the sector average is a bit over 1.2x, which is also the optimal level of debt for BDCs (i.e., it maximizes ROE without entering aggressive risk zone).

Final words

To earn the right to win or achieve great and durable income compounding results, our portfolios have to be based on assets that carry tangible entry yields, defensive fundamentals and income growth prospects.

In other words, these three have to be core strengths of their (portfolio asset) investment offering. The third aspect - income growth - is, however, a bit more nuanced as sometimes it might make sense to accept a below-inflation-rate income growth rate as long as it comes with higher entry yield, which, in turn, offers greater flexibility to make reinvestments and thus compound the income that way.

In my view, ENB and MAIN are definitely solid retirement income picks, which give us the right to win in the compounding journey.

data.title: BJ's Wholesale Club: Investing In Expansion, EPS Growth To Slow
data.description:
In our last column in the fall of 2025, we upgraded BJ's Wholesale Club Holdings, Inc. (BJ) to a Buy following a valuation reset and the projected growth. Shares were able to be grabbed at $87-$88 on the day of publication and rallied to over $102 in a few weeks. That is a solid trading return. Shares were a bit sideways since then but have dipped back toward $95 following the just-reported earnings, which we will discuss in this update column. What has changed is we have 2026 guidance, and that calls for pretty slow growth.

We think BJ shares are a Hold right now but would be compelled to move to a Buy once again if we retrace to the mid-$80s, where valuation relative to expected growth would make more sense. Let us discuss.

BJ's Wholesale Club Q4 Top-Line Figures And Margin Performance

Comparable sales are the key metric we watch for in retailers. Of course we also monitor total sales and margins, looking for expansion on both fronts. We like holding a current position as metrics are all healthy, but the growth is slow. Sure, the valuation is stretched for the growth, but it is a modest premium trading at a market multiple. We saw another quarter of year-over-year sales growth for BJ's, with revenues increasing 5.7% to $5.58 billion. And this surpassed consensus estimates slightly by $30 million. That is welcome news for longs. But more importantly, comparable sales were positive.

We want to reiterate that you should be aware that comparable sales here are influenced by fuel and gas prices, but the main driver is still merchandise margins and the price-to-sales mix. Including gasoline, total comparable club sales were up by 1.6% year-over-year, which is nice growth. Backing out the volatile fuel sales, comparable sales were up a strong 2.6%. We also saw great online comparable sales growth that was up 31% year-over-year.

The other thing we like about the model is that people need to pay just to walk in the doors. That is right. Not only does BJ's make money on its sales, of course, but to get in the door you need a membership. And membership continues to grow, with membership fee income rising 10.9%. Over 90% of existing members renewed in 2025 as well.

Then there are the margins, which we hone in on after examining total sales and comparable sales. These are showing strength as well and justify a buy, particularly on weakness in shares. It was a solid 5.5% increase in gross profit to $1.01 billion from $949.0 million compared to last year's Q4.

One blemish was that the merchandise gross margin rate (which backs out fuel sales and membership fee income) slipped by 50 basis points, but this was due to promotions and the sales mix. For the whole year, margins on merchandise were flat, which is fine. We next look to selling and administrative expenses. When revenues rise, we always expect these to rise. Well, they were up YOY $818.2 million versus $758.2 million a year ago. Keep in mind, though, that this still reflects new store openings. How did this translate to earnings power?

First, we should note that we saw a slight increase in the adjusted EBITDA line. It rose a paltry 0.7% to $266.5 million versus $264.6 million last year. So, here, clearly, it is slow growth, so we can understand why near-term investors are not rushing to buy. Net income was up to $125.9 million from $122.7 million, also due to the rising expenses. But these are investments for future growth. EPS, though, was a nice beat of $0.03, coming in at $0.96, up from $0.92. For the year, EPS was $4.38, a solid rise from $4.00.

Forward View

Why are shares down? We think it is the outlook, particularly for earnings. The projections are for stable but slow growth. And so, the Street may be reluctant to pay a market multiple right now at about $95 per share.

As we look ahead to 2026, that all-important comparable club sales metric (backing out fuel sales) is expected to increase 2.0% to 3.0% year-over-year. But with a high capex of $800 million, it is going to weigh on near-term earnings. We have to stress, however, that this is to generate future growth. A lot of that money is going toward new club openings and enhancements across the distribution network.

From an EPS standpoint, the $4.50 in expected EPS is only $0.12 per share growth for the year, or less than 3%. As such, we like a Hold here at these levels. We expect higher sales, positive comps, and increased membership, but for now the market multiple is still a bit rich for this level of growth.

data.title: LIT: Lithium And Battery Technology Remains Bullish (Downgrade)
data.description:
I rated the Global X Lithium & Battery Tech ETF (LIT) a buy in a May 21, 2025, Seeking Alpha article, where I concluded with the following:

Any investment in the LIT ETF should leave plenty of room to add on further declines, as it's virtually impossible to pick bottoms in any market. Meanwhile, prices tend to extend when searching for bottoms, making a longer-term scale-down approach to LIT optimal.

LIT was trading at $38.18 per share on May 20, 2025, and was in a bearish trend, making lower highs and lower lows since the November 2021 high of $97.13 per share. LIT shares more than doubled at the most recent February 2026 high of $78 per share, but the shares ran out of upside steam as EV sales and the Iran war weighed on lithium and battery metals prices. I had a buy rating on the LIT ETF in late May 2025 and am downgrading it to a hold in early March 2026. At $70 per share, LIT is still over 80% higher than the price on May 20, 2025.

Lithium And Battery Metal Demand Are Rising

Lithium is the lightest known metal with high electrochemical potential and low density. The leading modern use is in the production of high-performance batteries, but it also has applications in metallurgy, nuclear physics, and chemical synthesis.

Pure lithium metal serves as the anode in non-rechargeable batteries used for long-life devices such as pacemakers, watches, and cameras. Most lithium-ion batteries use lithium compounds rather than pure lithium, but next-generation lithium-metal batteries require pure lithium-metal anodes to achieve the highest possible energy density. Lithium is a critical ingredient in the lithium batteries for electric vehicles (EVs), phones, and grid storage.

The chart shows that Australia is the world’s leading lithium producer, followed by Chile and China.

A January 14, 2026, article in Carbon Credits forecasted strong lithium demand and higher prices in 2026. Lithium carbonate spot prices in China have climbed from $8,259 on June 23, 2025, to over $13,000 per ton in late November 2025. In March 2026, the spot price of battery-grade lithium was around $20,000 per ton. While EV demand may have declined over the past year, demand for energy storage systems for AI has soared.

As the chart shows, the lithium supply-demand equation has flipped from a surplus in 2025 to a deficit in 2026. The deficit is forecast to substantially rise over the coming decade. Meanwhile, demand for all battery metals will likely grow as AI energy demands increase over the coming years.

LIT Is A Lithium And Battery Technology ETF

The fund profile for the Global X Lithium & Battery Tech ETF (LIT) states:

At $69.20 per share, LIT had $1.65 billion in assets under management and trades an average of over 386,000 shares per day, making it a liquid ETF. LIT charges a 0.75% management fee, and its blended annual dividend of $0.31 per share yields 0.44%.

RIO Is LIT's Top Holding

The top holdings of the LIT ETF include:

My May 21, 2025, Seeking Alpha article on LIT highlighted the Rio Tinto (RIO)-Codelco joint venture developing a high-grade lithium project at Chile’s Salar de Maricunga. Chile is the world’s second-leading lithium-producing country. In late May, LIT’s leading holding was Albemarle Corporation (ALB), a U.S. lithium company. LIT had a 7.63% exposure to ALB on March 20, 2025. On March 5, 2026, LIT’s ALB exposure edged lower to 7.14%, but Rio Tinto (RIO) replaced ALB as LIT’s top holding with a 22.05% exposure. In late May 2025, I wrote, “The joint venture could eventually lead to RIO’s inclusion in the LIT ETF product.”

Higher Lows With High Volatility

The LIT ETF has been very volatile over the past few years.

The 20-year monthly chart highlights the LIT ETF's extreme volatility. After falling to a low of $17.83 per share in March 2020 when the global pandemic gripped markets, LIT rallied to a record high of $97.13 per share in November 2021, where it ran out of upside steam. LIT corrected 67.6% to a low of $31.44 in April 2025, where it found a bottom. The latest 148.1% rally took LIT to a high of $78 per share in February 2026. LIT has backed off from the recent high and was trading above $68 per share on March 5, 2026.

The monthly chart shows LIT remains in a bullish pattern, making higher lows since 2015, and while it made a lower high in February 2026, the trajectory remains positive. Moreover, the anticipated deficit in the global lithium market supports higher share prices for LIT’s holdings over the coming years. LIT is a volatile ETF, as lithium is a volatile commodity.

LIT's Seeking Alpha ETF Grades Are Mixed

Seeking Alpha’s latest ETF grades for LIT are as follows:

The rally in LIT since the April 2025 low earns it the highest possible A+ grade in the momentum category. LIT’s assets under management and daily trading volume earn it an A- in the liquidity category. The 0.75% management fee receives a D+ grade in expenses, while the dividend yields earn a D. The high risk and volatility of lithium producer shares receive a D- in the risk category.

Given the rally from April 2025 to the February high, I have changed my buy recommendation to a hold recommendation. However, given the deficit, the prospects for rising lithium prices over the coming years justify the current hold recommendation.

data.title: The AES Corporation (AES) Stock Analysis: Riding The AI Wave | 2-Minute Analysis
data.description:
Follow The AES Corporation on Seeking Alpha!

Learn more about Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings

Get Started With Seeking Alpha Premium Now

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Welcome to 2 Minute Analysis. Our goal is to not only entertain, but provide value and insights about the investments you care about. Today's episode is from this comment. So, let's throw 2 minutes on the clock and dive-in.

Today, we are diving into The AES Corporation, ticker symbol AES. Kicking things off here with the Quant rating system, it is currently a Hold rating on the stock, and we're going to dive into the Factor Grade metrics here in just a moment. Seeking Alpha Analysts currently have a Buy in aggregate on this stock, and that's from 2 analysts providing their coverage in the last 90-days.

Lastly, Wall Street analysts currently have a Buy on this stock as well, and that's from 14 analysts providing their coverage in the last 90-days. To learn more about how the Seeking Alpha Quant system and Seeking Alpha Analysts outperform the market, visit the link in the description of this video.

Now, let's dive deeper. This is currently a $12.31 billion market capitalization company found within the utility sector and the independent power producers and energy traders industry. First things first, jumping into this Valuation grade, which is currently an A, we can see that the PEG non-GAAP forward ratio is currently 0.83, compared to the sector at 2.66, showcasing almost a 68.5% discount to the sector.

Jumping into the Growth grade currently a D-, that EPS GAAP growth forward is currently sitting at 79.17%, compared to the sector at just 7.89%. Also want to highlight the operating cash flow growth year-over-year is 63.50%, compared to the sector at just 4.14%. Very favorable metric there.

Jumping into the Profitability is currently a C grade. We can look at a return on common equity sitting at 30.16%, compared to the sector at just 9.36%. And they do have a nice cash from operations over the trailing 12-months of 3.91 billion, compared to the sector at just 1.44 billion.

Jumping into the Momentum grade that is currently an A+, pretty favorable across the board here, obviously, with this company being within the power producers industry, 66.47% compared to the sector at just 24.06% riding the AI wave.

Jumping into the Revisions grade, currently a C-. That is 3 up revisions and 3 down revisions for earnings per share over the last three months, and 3 up revisions and 1 down revision for revenue numbers over the last three months.

Now that this is a utility, it does pay a dividend. You will see the dividend yield is 4.07%. Dividend safety grade is currently a B-. The dividend growth is a D. The dividend yield is an A grade, and the dividend consistency is a B-.

Jumping in here, we can see over consistency, they have been paying their dividend for 13 years and growing it for 12 years. And if we take a quick look at the dividend yield, it is currently beating its sector on that yield, which is nice and favorable for shareholders.

Obviously, want to highlight as well, AES Corporation is taking a little bit of a hit today. So, if you want to get all the free breaking news alerts about what's going on with this company right now, come here to Seeking Alpha. Click this follow button here at the top of the page. You'll get free breaking news alerts on The AES Corporation and any other symbol that you want to follow here on Seeking Alpha.

And that's going to wrap it up for this episode. If you have a ticker you want us to cover, add it to the comment section below, and don't forget to follow Seeking Alpha so you get notified when the next 2 Minute Analysis gets published.

data.title: FNDA: Small Value ETF Beating The Benchmark But Lagging Some Peers
data.description:
This article updates my review of September 2025 in light of current holdings and recent performance.

FNDA strategy

Schwab Fundamental US Small Company ETF (FNDA) was launched on 08/15/2013 and tracks the RAFI Fundamental High Liquidity U.S. Small Index. FNDA has 889 holdings, a 30-day SEC yield of 1.24%, and an expense ratio of 0.25%. Distributions are paid quarterly. FNDA is part of a series of ETFs based on the RAFI methodology, also including:

  • Schwab Fundamental US Broad Market Index ETF (FNDB)
  • Schwab Fundamental US Large Company Index ETF (FNDX)
  • Schwab Fundamental International Equity ETF (FNDF)
  • Schwab Fundamental International Small Equity ETF (FNDC)
  • Schwab Fundamental Emerging Markets Equity ETF (FNDE)

As described in the prospectus by Charles Schwab Asset Management:

The index selects, ranks, and weights securities by fundamental measures of company size – adjusted sales, retained operating cash flow, and dividends plus buybacks – rather than market capitalization.

This methodology based on a fundamental size score is common to all RAFI indexes. The starting universe is composed of the U.S. companies in the RAFI Global Equity Investable Universe. The bottom 12.5% of the companies by cumulative score are included in the underlying index. The index is divided into four equal segments rebalanced quarterly, resulting in an annual complete rebalancing. The portfolio’s turnover rate was 26% in the most recent fiscal year. I will use as a benchmark the Russell 2000 index, represented by iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM).

Portfolio

The fund is diversified across sectors, with notable exposure in industrials (20.5% of asset value), financials (16.5%), consumer discretionary (13.6%), and technology (11.4%). Other sectors are below 10%. Compared to the Russell 2000, FNDA greatly downplays healthcare.

The portfolio has low company-specific risk. The top 10 issuers, listed in the next table, represent only 5.2% of asset value, and the heaviest position weighs about 1%.

FNDA is second to last in total return and Sharpe ratio (a measure of risk-adjusted performance) over the past four years. The best performers in the list are the actively managed ETFs AVUV and DFSV. FNDA’s closest competitor, PRFZ, outperforms it by 67 bps annualized with similar volatility. However, FNDA and PRFZ are almost tied since FNDA’s inception:

Takeaway

Schwab Fundamental US Small Company ETF has a portfolio of 889 small caps based on a fundamental size score. FNDA has value characteristics and great diversification across sectors and holdings. FNDA has outperformed the Russell 2000 since inception and shows excellent dividend growth, even though the yield is low. However, the fund has lagged several small-value ETFs, such as AVUV, DFSV, and VBR, since 2022.
